text: The video shows a large intramural myoma located in the lower posterior right wall of the uterus.
The surgeon creates a small stab wound in the right hypochondriac region using an 11-blade knife, preparing for port placement in the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.

The surgeon introduces the Veress needle, angling it towards the stomach. After hearing three clicks indicating entry, a syringe is used to perform irrigation, suction, and hanging drop tests to confirm the needle is inside the abdominal cavity. The hanging drop test shows the drop being sucked down as the abdominal wall is lifted.
The surgeon attaches the tubing to the insufflator and observes the insufflator readings. Insufflation begins, creating a pneumoperitoneum for laparoscopic access.
The insufflator displays a preset pressure of eighteen, with the actual pressure gradually increasing to nineteen as 1.8 liters of gas are introduced into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon removes the varus needle and enlarges the incision to 11 mm. An optical trocar is inserted, pointed towards the stomach. The trocar is then removed, and a telescope is introduced into the cannula to begin the initial diagnostic laparoscopy, preparing for the myomectomy surgery.
The surgeon visually identifies a large intramural myoma located on the posterior uterine wall, extending towards the anterior wall. The fallopian tube is visible adjacent to the myoma.
The posterior wall of the uterus is visible, with a large intramural myoma occupying almost the entire uterine cavity.
The video shows the intramural myoma occupying the posterior wall of the uterus, extending towards the anterior wall.
The video shows the portion of the myoma located on the posterior lateral wall of the uterus, highlighting its position and extent within the uterine structure.
The surgeon measures the myoma, which is located on the posterior wall of the uterus, and notes its size as thirteen centimeters.
The surgeon visually identifies the right fallopian tube adjacent to the large intramural myoma located on the posterior wall of the uterus.
The surgeon visually identifies the right ovary, located adjacent to the previously mentioned fallopian tube and posterior lateral wall of the uterus.
The intestines are visible in the surgical field, adjacent to the posterior lateral wall where the intramural myoma is located.
The surgeon identifies the triangle of dome, a visible anatomical landmark during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ureter on the right side, visible within the surgical field.

The peristalsis of the right ureter is visible. The common iliac and external iliac vessels are identified. The sacral promontory is shown, and the small bowel is lifted to reveal the aorta. The infundibulopelvic ligament is visible above the ureter. Both the triangle of doom and the triangle of pain are clearly seen.
The surgeon injects vasopressin diluted with normal saline for hydro dissection. A myoma screw holds the uterus as the posterior wall is cut longitudinally.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to extend a longitudinal incision approximately six to seven centimeters on the posterior wall of the uterus. The muscle layers are spread laterally, and the myoma screw is positioned to hold the tissue.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw to secure the fibroid and extends the incision further on the uterine wall. Suction irrigation is employed to facilitate enucleation, performing maximum blunt dissection around the fibroid.

The surgeon uses a 10 mm stone holding forceps to gently separate the myoma from the surrounding tissue, revealing a clear dissection plane as the fibroid gradually emerges from the uterine wall. This process is slow and meticulous, emphasizing the use of blunt dissection techniques.

The surgeon separates the myoma from surrounding tissues, reaching the falciform ligament on the right side. Muscle fibers obstructing the myoma's removal are visible, and the harmonic device is used to divide these fibers, facilitating the extraction of the entire myoma.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to prevent damage to the ascending uterine artery while lifting the myoma with a stone holding forceps. The myoma is carefully extracted, ensuring the pedicle does not damage the surrounding anatomical structures, including the triangle of doom and the cecum.
The surgeon has successfully separated the entire myoma from surrounding tissues, completing the dissection process.
The surgeon begins suturing the posterior wall after the removal of the myoma, using precise needle placement to close the incision site.
The surgeon applies a couple of mix mistra knots to the internal layer of the muscles to approximate the muscle layer, providing tension sutures, followed by intracorporeal suturing.

The surgeon performs extracorporeal knot tying using a baseball suture technique to tightly approximate the posterior muscle layer. The Mishra's knot is tied with a sequence of one hitch, followed by one wind and lock, then a second wind and lock.

The surgeon uses a Vandarkar knot pusher to tightly approximate the sutures, applying tension to secure the muscle layer. The assistant holds the large suture tail to lift it, while additional tension sutures are placed in the deep muscle layer.
The surgeon includes the serous layer in the suturing process to provide tension over the uterus, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses the Vandarkar knot pusher to tighten the knot after bringing the suture out, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of Misra's knot, a secure extracorporeal knot, to apply tension sutures for approximating the muscle layer during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon ties the third Misra's knot to approximate the muscle layer, using a knot pusher to ensure secure tension on the suture.
The surgeon begins intracorporeal suturing on the uterine muscle layer, following the placement of tension sutures using Misra's knot and Vandarkar knot pusher.
The surgeon combines extracorporeal and intracorporeal suturing techniques to secure the uterine muscle, using visible knots to maintain tension.
The surgeon ties another extracorporeal knot using a suture, securing it with a knot pusher to maintain tension and approximate the muscle tissue.
The surgeon tightens the extracorporeal Misra's knot to secure the tension suture, approximating the muscle tissue.
The surgical field shows a small collection of old blood, which will be removed using suction after the procedure.
The surgeon ties a third extracorporeal knot and then begins intracorporeal suturing, combining both techniques in the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a Dhandi jamming knot as a starter suture, followed by the initiation of continuous suturing.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing, occasionally interlocking the suture in the middle of the process.
The surgeon performs a longitudinal incision on the posterior wall myoma, avoiding a transverse cut, and emphasizes the need for secure suturing of the incision site.
The surgeon performs multiple-layer suturing using number one Vicryl, with the suture assistant holding the tail of the suture to facilitate the process.

The surgeon terminates the intracorporeal suture using a number one Vicryl after reaching the limit of 20 to 30 centimeters. The tail of the extracorporeal knot is used to secure the suture, and the surgeon prepares to continue suturing with a new suture, employing the 'C' and reverse 'C' technique to tighten the suture.

The surgeon applies multiple layers of sutures to the myoma, using a combination of extracorporeal and intracorporeal techniques. Several sutures are visible being placed to ensure secure closure of the incision.
The surgeon begins suturing the posterior wall myoma using number one Vicryl, continuing the process of multiple layer suturing with careful handling of the suture tail by the assistant.
The surgeon places a deeper suture in the upper layer of the uterine tissue, using a technique that involves multiple layers of suturing.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing using a Dandy jamming knot technique, securing the uterine tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a pre-tied loop to secure the suture in place during the repair of the uterine tissue.
The surgeon takes a bite of tissue with the needle, passes it through the pre-tied loop, and pulls it to secure the suture.
The surgeon pulls the suture, causing the pre-tied loop to automatically lock, securing the tissue layers.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the last layer, identified as the serous layer, with full thickness being sutured.
The surgeon completes the repair of the uterus, with the sutured layers visibly intact and the uterine structure restored.
The surgeon ensures the protection of the fallopian tube during the myomectomy procedure, with minimal blood loss observed, estimated between 200 to 300 ml.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing on the last layer of the uterus, completing the repair process.
The surgeon terminates the continuous suture in the uterus by tying it off with the initial tail of the extracorporeal knot.
The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the suture, maintaining tension as the continuous suture is applied to the uterine tissue.
The assistant holds the ovarian ligament tightly to ensure good approximation of the sutured layers, while the surgeon prepares to finalize the suture termination using the tail of Mishra's knot.
The surgeon holds the needle due to the small size of the suture, while cautioning against this practice to prevent potential damage to the iliac vessel.
The surgeon tightens the final suture, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon cuts and removes the suture tail after completing the final stitch.
The surgeon cuts the suture and removes the needle using an endoske needle through a 5mm port. The procedure transitions to morselation of a 13-centimeter myoma, which is a time-consuming process.

The surgeon uses an electrical morcellator to perform morcellation, directing the device towards the anterior abdominal wall for safety. The morcellation is conducted slowly and carefully, ensuring safe removal of the myoma tissue.
The surgeon holds a tenaculum, a surgical instrument used for grasping and holding tissues during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a tenaculum to securely hold the myoma, preparing to perform slow and careful morselation of the tissue.
The surgeon positions the tissue close to the morcellator to facilitate the morcellation process, ensuring efficient removal of the myoma.
The surgeon carefully positions the morcellator to avoid sliding over the myoma, preventing accidental damage to the iliac vessel or small bowel.
The surgeon continues the slow and careful morselation of the myoma using the morseletan, ensuring the instrument is directed safely towards the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon carefully performs morselation while ensuring the small intestine, visible nearby, remains unharmed.
The surgeon carefully extracts morcellated tissue, ensuring that surrounding structures, such as the bowel, are not accidentally morcellated during the procedure.
The surgeon extracts the myoma, which is visibly emerging from the surgical site.
The surgeon continues to morcellate the remaining tissue, carefully fragmenting it within the surgical field.
The surgeon handles an 18-centimeter sub-serous and pedunculated myoma, which is visible as a large, protruding mass attached to the uterine surface.
The surgeon removes the sub-serous and pedunculated myoma, which is visibly detached from the uterine wall, allowing for easy extraction.
The surgeon uses a tenaculum or myoma screw to securely hold the myoma during the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs morselation on the myoma, with visible irritation of the tissue to expedite the process.
The surgeon ensures that all fragmented tissue from the morselation process is carefully removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon uses stone holding forceps to remove fragmented tissue pieces scattered throughout the surgical field.
The surgeon performs final irrigation and suction in the surgical field, clearing any remaining tissue fragments and fluids.
The surgeon suctions out collected blood from the surgical field and places a drain into the posterior cul-de-sac.

The surgeon uses suction to remove residual blood and tissue fragments, paying attention to areas near Morrison's pouch. The procedure progresses to closing the 15mm port used for the morcellator, ensuring proper closure to prevent hernia formation.
The surgeon performs the final port closure, completing the myomectomy procedure.
